Farn Polrrrcel PRACrrcEs C orraurssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 e Fax (916) 322-0886
March 15,2007
Catherine L. DiCamillo
CityAttorney
1901 Airport Road, Suite 300
South Lake Tahoe, Califom ia 96150-7 004
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-026
Dear Ms. DiCamillo:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of South Lake Tahoe
City Councilmember Jerry Birdwell regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the'Act').r This letteiis based on the facts present"J. fn" f,ui.
Political Practices Commission (the "Commission') does not act as the finder of fact
when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FppC Ops. 71.)
QUESTTON
Does Councilmember Birdwell have a conflict of interest prohibiting him from
participating in decisions by the City of South Lake Tahoe to provide fundiig to either
the Lake Tahoe Visitors Authority or any of several local Chambers of Comierce from
monies raised through the City,s Transient Occupancy Taxes?
CONCLUSION
No. Under the facts presented, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, under the applicablathresholds, on any of
Councilmember Birdwell's economic interests identified herein. Therefore, the Act does
not prohibit Councilmember Birdwell from participating in decisions by the City of South
Lake Tahoe to provide funding to either the Lake tafroe Visitors Authority or any of
I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear attitle 2,sectiors
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-07-026
Page No. 2
several local Chambers of Commerce from monies raised through the City,s Transient
Occupancy Taxes.
FACTS
Councilmember Birdwell owns a 50 percent interest in a lodging property and
b-usiness in the city of south Lake Tahoe (the ,,city,)known as Black Biarinn (the
"Inn'). The Inn has nine rental units, and rental rooms include breakfast and awine and
cheese reception. The Inn functions as a bed and breakfast type of lodging facility. Most
of Inn's business is generated through the efforts of the Inn, with most oflhe revenue
coming from guests solicited directly by the owners of the business, rather than those
obtained through advertising services of the Lake Tahoe Visitors Authority (the
"LTVA') or any Chamber of Commerce.
The revenue nambers for the past three years have been obtained by council-
member Birdwell by including a section in his registration sheets for the guests to note
how they learned of the Inn and compiling the doilar values associated with the stay
which guests attribute to either the LTVA or the Chamber of Commerce. Those figures
are:
*revenues generated by
CouncilmemberBirdwell has owned a 50 percent interest in the Inn forthe past
eight years, during which time he has served on thl board of directors of the Chamber of
Commerce. He is not presently serving on the board of directors of any of the Chambers
of Commerce that operate within the City, and he does not serye on the Board of
Directors of the LTVA.
The LTVA was formed around 1985 as a Joint Powers Agency of the City, and
the County of Douglas, Nevada, to promote tourism in the Soutl Sfroie portion of the
Lake Tahoe Basin- The LTVA is composed ofrepresentatives from the?ity; Douglas
County, Nevada; the local Chamber of Co**r""e^; and a local lodgng assoliation. The
LTVA seeks fundingtom the City each year. In the most recent ttr"" y"*, the City
granted the LTVA's funding request in the following amounts:
2405/2006 _ $225,000
2004/2005 _ s225,000
2003t2ao4 _ $518,000
In the past three fiscal years the South Lake Tahoe Chamber of Commerce
requested and received funding from the City as follows:
Black Bear Inn

200s
2004
Total Revenues
$331,000.00
$272,000.00
$222,000.00
Lake Tahoe Visitors
$ 
0.00

420.00
$ 
0.00
Chamber of Commerce*
$ 1,815.00
$ 
76s.00
$ 3,530.00


FileNo. A-07-026
Page No. 3
2005/2006 _ $ 93,018
2004/2005 _$101,474
2003t2004 _$122,474
Last Fall, the South Lake Tahoe Chamber of Commerce merged with a Nevada
entity- the Tahoe-Douglas chamber of commerce, formirg a rr"* South Tahoe
Chamber of Commerce. This organization has requested funding from the City.
Additionally, a new Chamber of Commerce - 
the South Lake Tahoe Chamber of
Commerce has also requested funding from the City. It is anticipated that the city council
will be receiving the annual requests for funding in the roy.reui future, with the funding
requests of the LTVA and the two Chambers of Commerce coming before the city
council at its March 6, 2007,meeting.
Every lodging property in the City is listed in the LTVA's major publications and
on its website. The LTVA provides a monthly newsletter, via e-mail, that highlights one
or two lodging properties. The LTVA provides an opportunity to each lodging property
to be highlighted in a monthlynewsletter for a fee of $400. The opportunity is available
to every lodging property and is sold on a first-come, first-served basis. Councilmember
Birdwell's business has participated in this monthly newsletter service twice in the past
eight years, most recently in the surnmer of 2006.
The City obtains approximately 5.5 million of its 28 million dollar General Fund
budget from Transient Occupancy Taxes, which are currently ten percent of lodging
properties' gross revenues from the rental of their rooms.
ANALYSIS
Porrxrmr, Conrr,rcr or INTEREsT
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will ..perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them.' (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making , pxticipating in making or
otherwise using his orher official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. @egulation 18700(b).) The general
rule, however, is that a conflict of,interest exists whenever a public official makes a
Sovemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more ofhis or her financial interests.
srsps 1 & 2 : Is coUNcTLMEMBER Brnownr,r, A puslIc orrrcrnr, Manoxc,
PanrtcplTlNc rN MarcNc, oR TNFLTTENCTNG A GovrnxurENTAL DECrsroN?
As a member of the South Lake Tahoe City Council, Councilmember Birdwell is a
public official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently he may not make,


FileNo. A-07-026
Page No. 4
participate in making or otherwise use his official position to influence any decisions that
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of his economic
interests. Councilmember Birdwell will be called upon to consider whether the City should
approve or disapprove of the expenditure of funds received from the City,s Transient
Occupancy Taxes to various Chambers of Commerce and the Lake Tahoe Visitors
Authority. Therefore, he will be making,participating in making, or otherwise using his
official position to influence a govemmental decision.
srrp 3: Dops couxcrr.urvmn D,wrs rrAvE A porENTrALLy DrseuAr,rrv1Nc
Ecoxoprrc INrrnesr?
a
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); reg. lg7o3.l(a)); or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trusteg employee, or holds any position of
management (section 87 1 03(d); reg. I 8703. I (b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section S7103(b); reg. 1g703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to the dicision (section SZt O:1";;
reg. 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within l2 months prior to the decision (section g7l 03(e); ,"g. lslTz .+1;
a
a
o
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects', rule
(section 87 103; reg. I 8703.5).
Forpurposes of section 87l}3,"indirect invesffnent or interest means any
invesfine,nt or interest owned by the spouse of dependent child of a public official, ... or
by a business entity or trust in which the ofEcial, the official,s agenis, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, orbeneficially a l0-perc*iirt"rot o.
greater." (Section 871 03.)
Your request does not present any facts indicating that Councilmernber Birdwell
has any economic interests under other than those related to his ownership interest in the
Inn lodging property. Accordingly, our analysis is limited to Council- member
Birdwell's economic interest arising from his ownership of the Inn and its property.


FileNo. A-07-026
Page No. 5
You have indicated that Councilmember Birdwell has a 50-percent ownership
interest in the Inn. We assumed that this interest is worth $2,000 oi 11or" and that hi is
also a "director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
with the business." Therefore, Councilmember Birdwell had an Lconomic interest in the
Inn under both sections 87103 (a) and (d). Additionally, according to the figures you
have provided, Councilmember Birdwell will have received $500 or more ii income
during the 12 months prior to the governmental decision. Therefore, Councilmember
Birdwell has an additional economic interest in the Inn as a source of income to him.
As an ownetr with a ten-percent or greater interest in the business, Councilmember
Birdwell also has a source of income economic interest in each of the customers of the
Inn from whom he receives $500 or more as his pro rata share of the income of the
business. (Sections 82030, 871 03(c).)
Finally, Councilmember Birdwell has an economic interest in the real property on
which the Inn is located, either as a direct owner of the property, or as an indirect owner
through the business because he owns a 10-percent or greater interest in the business.
srnp 4: rs Tm Ecoxourc rxrrnpsrDrREcTly oR rNDrREcrLyINvoL\rrD rN Tm
Govrnxvmxrar, DrclsroN ?
"In order to determine if a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
govemmental decision. " (Regulation 1 8704(a).)
for
whether or not a
Regulation t8704.t(a) provides the rule
is directly or
indirectly
in a decision:
entity or a source of income
'.A person, including business entities, sources of incomg and sources of
gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Lritiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named pfiy in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning
the decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuancq renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any licensg permit, or otLer entitlement
to, or contract with, the subject pe,rson.',
The governmental decisions about which you have inquired concern funding
requests by the LTVA and two local Chambers of Commerce. Under the facts yoo hur"
presented, there is nothing to indicate that any of these organizations qualifu as a source
of income econornic interest to CouncilmemberBirdwell. Furthermore, the Inn is not an


File No. A-07-026
Page No. 6
applicant or named party or the subject of the proceeding under regulation 1S70a.l(a)(l)
or Q). Therefore, Councilmember Birdwell's economic interest in the Inn is indirectly 
-
involved in the decision, and unless he has any economic interest in any of the
organizations requesting funding, he has no other business entity or source of income
economic interests that are directly involved in the decision.
Again, because you have not provided any facts indicating any other economic
interests, including guests who are sources of income, our analysis is limited to the
economic interest identifi ed above.
Regulation I 8704. I (b) provides:
"(b) ... If a business entity source of income, or source of a gift is not
directly involved in a govemmental decision, apply the materiality
standards in California Code of Regulations, Title 2, section 18705.1(c),
section I 8705.3(b), or section I S705.4(b), respectively.,,
Real Propertv: Regulation l8704.2lists the factors that determine whether an
economic interest in real property is directly or indirectly involved in a governmental
decision. Real property that does not fall into one of the criteria listed ildeemed to be
indirectly involved in the decision. Accordingly, Councilmember Birdwell's real
property is indirectly involved in the decision.
Srrp 5: M.q,TERrALrrySraNnmo
Business Entitv and Source of Income from Business Entity: For indirectly
involved business entities, and for indirectly involved sources of income that are business
entities (regulation 18705.3(b)(1)), the materiality standard is provided in regulation
18705.1(c):
"(c) Indirectly involved business entities. The following materiality
standards apply when a business entity in which a public official has an
economic interest is indirectly involved in a govemmental decision. If
more than one of the following subdivisions is applicable to the business
entity in question, apply the subdivision with the highest dollar
thresholds."
For relatively small business entities (those not listed on tle New York or
American Stock Exchange, or on NASDAQ, and, if not so listed, with net income of less
than $500,000 for its most recent fiscal year) the materiality standard set forth in
subdivision I 8705. I (c)(4) applies:
'(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)(l)-(3), the
financial eflect of a governmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:


File No. A-07-026
Page No. 7
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminaiing
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,
Real Property: For governmental decisions that affect economic interests in real
property, the materiality standard set forth in regulation 18705.2 applies. (Regulation
1 87 05 (a)Q).) Regulation I 8705.2(bX I ) states :
"(b) Indirectly involved real property interests.
"(1) Real property, other than leaseholds. The financial efiflect of a
governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the
governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption
may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding
the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make
it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on the real property in which the public official has an interest. ...,,
If the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision on the Inn meets the
thresholds identified abovg the financial effect is considered material.
Srrp 6: Rr,lsonarr,yFoREsEEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable', if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. a-nnanciat effect need
not be certun to be considered reasonably foreseeablg but it rnust be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops.i98.)
Given the facts you have presented, including the fact thatthe mostreyenue generated by either
the LTVd a local Chamber of Commerce, or a combination of both in the tast three iscal years is less than
one-tenth the amount necessary to meet the materiality threshold identified herein, it appears irrprobable
that there will be a substantial likelihood of a material financial effect on the Inn u* nr"i.lt of any of the
decisions. This is further based on the fact that you have not indicated anything about the "*rt 
fimding
requests that may give result to a different financial effect on Councilmember Birdwell,s economic interest
than in the past. We leave this factor for your consideration; the Cornmission does not act as a finder of
fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (lg71) I FppC Ops. 71.)


File No. A-07-026
Page No. 8
Sffips 7 ANn 8: Purr,rc GrNrnnr.r,y& Lncar,Ly RJeurREn pmrrcrpATloN
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally,, or
"legally required participation" exceptions would be applicable herein. AJcordingiy, we
have not provided an analysis involving those steps. Ilyou do find that additional facts
might indicate that it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality threshold will be met
and you wish further advice on the public generally exception, pllase feel free to write
agun.
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

economic interest in real property is directly or indirectly involved in a governmental
decision. Real property that does not fall into one of the criteria listed ildeemed to be
indirectly involved in the decision. Accordingly, Councilmember Birdwell's real
property is indirectly involved in the decision.
Srrp 5: M.q,TERrALrrySraNnmo
Business Entitv and Source of Income from Business Entity: For indirectly
involved business entities, and for indirectly involved sources of income that are business
entities (regulation 18705.3(b)(1)), the materiality standard is provided in regulation
18705.1(c):
"(c) Indirectly involved business entities. The following materiality
standards apply when a business entity in which a public official has an
economic interest is indirectly involved in a govemmental decision. If
more than one of the following subdivisions is applicable to the business
entity in question, apply the subdivision with the highest dollar
thresholds."
For relatively small business entities (those not listed on tle New York or
American Stock Exchange, or on NASDAQ, and, if not so listed, with net income of less
than $500,000 for its most recent fiscal year) the materiality standard set forth in
subdivision I 8705. I (c)(4) applies:
'(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)(l)-(3), the
financial eflect of a governmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminaiing
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,
Real Property: For governmental decisions that affect economic interests in real
property, the materiality standard set forth in regulation 18705.2 applies. (Regulation
1 87 05 (a)Q).) Regulation I 8705.2(bX I ) states :
"(b) Indirectly involved real property interests.
"(1) Real property, other than leaseholds. The financial efiflect of a
governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the
governmental decision is presumed not to be material. This presumption
may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding
the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make
it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on the real property in which the public official has an interest. ...,,
If the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision on the Inn meets the
thresholds identified abovg the financial effect is considered material.
Srrp 6: Rr,lsonarr,yFoREsEEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable', if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. a-nnanciat effect need
not be certun to be considered reasonably foreseeablg but it rnust be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops.i98.)
Given the facts you have presented, including the fact thatthe mostreyenue generated by either
the LTVd a local Chamber of Commerce, or a combination of both in the tast three iscal years is less than
one-tenth the amount necessary to meet the materiality threshold identified herein, it appears irrprobable
that there will be a substantial likelihood of a material financial effect on the Inn u* nr"i.lt of any of the
decisions. This is further based on the fact that you have not indicated anything about the "*rt 
fimding
requests that may give result to a different financial effect on Councilmember Birdwell,s economic interest
than in the past. We leave this factor for your consideration; the Cornmission does not act as a finder of
fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (lg71) I FppC Ops. 71.)
Sffips 7 ANn 8: Purr,rc GrNrnnr.r,y& Lncar,Ly RJeurREn pmrrcrpATloN
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally,, or
"legally required participation" exceptions would be applicable herein. AJcordingiy, we
have not provided an analysis involving those steps. Ilyou do find that additional facts

QUESTION: Does Councilmember Birdwell have a conflict of interest prohibiting him from
participating in decisions by the City of South Lake Tahoe to provide fundiig to either
the Lake Tahoe Visitors Authority or any of several local Chambers of Comierce from
monies raised through the City,s Transient Occupancy Taxes?

CONCLUSION: No. Under the facts presented, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, under the applicablathresholds, on any of
Councilmember Birdwell's economic interests identified herein. Therefore, the Act does
not prohibit Councilmember Birdwell from participating in decisions by the City of South
Lake Tahoe to provide funding to either the Lake tafroe Visitors Authority or any of
several local Chambers of Commerce from monies raised through the City,s Transient
Occupancy Taxes.

QUESTTON Does Councilmember Birdwell have a conflict of interest prohibiting him from participating in decisions by the City of South Lake Tahoe to provide fundiig to either the Lake Tahoe Visitors Authority or any of several local Chambers of Comierce from monies raised through the City,s Transient Occupancy Taxes? CONCLUSION No. Under the facts presented, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, under the applicablathresholds, on any of Councilmember Birdwell's economic interests identified herein. FACTS Councilmember Birdwell owns a 50 percent interest in a lodging property and b-usiness in the city of south Lake Tahoe (the ,,city,)known as Black Biarinn (the "Inn'). The Inn has nine rental units, and rental rooms include breakfast and awine and cheese reception. The Inn functions as a bed and breakfast type of lodging facility. Most of Inn's business is generated through the efforts of the Inn, with most oflhe revenue coming from guests solicited directly by the owners of the business, rather than those obtained through advertising services of the Lake Tahoe Visitors Authority (the "LTVA') or any Chamber of Commerce. The revenue nambers for the past three years have been obtained by council- member Birdwell by including a section in his registration sheets for the guests to note how they learned of the Inn and compiling the doilar values associated with the stay which guests attribute to either the LTVA or the Chamber of Commerce. Those figures are: *revenues generated by CouncilmemberBirdwell has owned a 50 percent interest in the Inn forthe past eight years, during which time he has served on thl board of directors of the Chamber of Commerce. He is not presently serving on the board of directors of any of the Chambers of Commerce that operate within the City, and he does not serye on the Board of Directors of the LTVA.
